title: AI Tools and Your VA Claim: Hallucination Risks, Privacy Traps, and the Claim Shark Pipeline
excerpt: Veterans are turning to AI tools to draft nexus letters and navigate 38 CFR. But fabricated citations, PII exposure, and predatory consultants make this a minefield. Here's how to tell a good AI tool from a dangerous one.
author: Chris Combs
body:
Every veteran filing a VA disability claim faces the same wall: thousands of pages of regulations, a schedule of rating disabilities that reads like a medical textbook, and a process designed by bureaucrats who never had to use it. Artificial intelligence promises to break through that wall. Tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and specialized platforms like VetClaims.ai can translate 38 CFR into plain English, draft personal statements, and generate nexus letter frameworks in minutes.

But not all AI tools are created equal. Some will help you build a stronger claim. Others will fabricate evidence, expose your private medical records, and cost you years in denied appeals. The difference comes down to three things: accuracy, privacy, and accountability. Here's how to evaluate any AI tool before you trust it with your claim.

## :search: The Hallucination Problem: When AI Fabricates Your Evidence

Large language models do not understand law. They predict text. In the VA claims context, this distinction is critical. A general-purpose AI tool -- one not specifically built for veterans law -- will routinely fabricate 38 CFR section numbers, invent diagnostic codes that don't exist, and generate nexus rationales citing medical studies that were never published.

This is not a bug. It's how the technology works. When an AI doesn't have the answer, it generates something that looks right. In casual conversation, that's a minor annoyance. In a VA claim, it's a legal landmine.

> [!warning] VA raters require nexus opinions to state a condition is 'at least as likely as not (50% or greater probability)' connected to service. General AI tools often substitute 'probably caused by' or 'may be related to' -- language that sounds close but is legally insufficient. That single word choice is the difference between a granted claim and an immediate denial.

The consequences compound. Submitting a claim with fabricated legal citations doesn't just get denied -- it can permanently damage your credibility in the claims file. Once a rater flags evidence as unreliable, every subsequent submission faces heightened scrutiny. A letter drafted by AI in seconds can trigger an appeal process that takes years.

### What a Good AI Tool Gets Right

- Every 38 CFR citation maps to an actual regulation you can verify at ecfr.gov
- Diagnostic codes match the VA Schedule for Rating Disabilities exactly
- Nexus language defaults to 'at least as likely as not' -- never 'may,' 'could,' or 'probably'
- Medical literature references link to real, published studies
- Range of Motion values reflect actual clinical standards, not generated approximations

## :lock: Privacy: The Risk You Don't See

To get useful output from any AI tool, you have to give it context. For VA claims, that means uploading some of the most sensitive documents you own: C-files, DD-214s, medical records with diagnoses and treatment histories, and personal statements describing traumatic experiences.

The question every veteran should ask before uploading anything: where does this data go?

Most commercial AI platforms retain your input data. Some use it to train future models -- meaning your private medical history could influence responses generated for other users. Most have no HIPAA obligations and no VA oversight. Your protected health information and military service records are being processed by systems with terms of service written for general consumers, not veterans handling sensitive government records.

> [!important] The risk multiplies when paid consultants enter the picture. Some use speech-to-text programs to transcribe veteran phone calls, then feed those transcripts into standard AI subscriptions. This creates multiple copies of your sensitive data across multiple third-party servers, none bound by government data protection standards.

### What a Good AI Tool Gets Right

- Your data is never used to train models -- non-training agreements by default
- Automatic detection and redaction of PII before processing
- No retention of uploaded medical records or service documents after your session
- Clear documentation of where your data is stored and who can access it
- Compliance with federal data protection standards, not just consumer-grade privacy policies

## :money: The Claim Shark Pipeline: $1,000 for a $20 Product

The most predatory pattern in the VA claims space right now is the industrialization of AI-generated nexus letters. The playbook is simple: set up a professional-looking website, run TikTok and Facebook ads targeting veterans, charge $1,000 or more per nexus letter, and generate the content using a $20/month ChatGPT subscription.

These operations -- sometimes called 'nexus mills' -- produce high-volume, low-quality letters without genuine medical analysis. The letters follow a template. The AI fills in the blanks. No physician actually reviews your specific medical history against the rating criteria. No one checks whether the diagnostic code matches your actual condition. And when the claim gets denied, the consultant is already on to the next customer.

California has already responded. Governor Newsom signed a consumer protection bill specifically targeting veteran claim fraud. But legislation moves slowly, and the pipeline keeps growing.

> [!tip] The underlying AI technology costs roughly $20 per month. A veteran who learns to use it directly -- combined with free VSO assistance -- can produce better results than a consultant charging fifty times that amount.

### Red Flags for Predatory Services

- Fees exceeding $500 for a nexus letter or claim review
- Guarantees of specific rating percentages
- Pressure to sign up immediately through social media ads
- Inability to explain their methodology beyond 'AI-powered'
- No accreditation with VA Office of General Counsel
- No named physician reviewing and signing the final letter

## :gavel: What a Responsible AI Tool Actually Looks Like

Not every AI tool is a threat. The technology genuinely can help veterans navigate the claims process faster and more effectively. The key is knowing what separates a trustworthy tool from a dangerous one.

**Accuracy:** The tool grounds every citation in actual 38 CFR text, M21-1 adjudication manual entries, or published CAVC decisions. It doesn't generate speculative language. It doesn't fabricate references. When it doesn't know something, it says so instead of making something up.

**Privacy:** Non-training data agreements are the default, not a premium feature. The tool automatically redacts PII. It doesn't retain your uploaded medical records or service documents. You can see exactly where your data goes and delete it when you're done.

**Accountability:** Every piece of generated content comes with a clear disclaimer that it requires human review before submission to VA. The tool is designed to assist your research, not replace your judgment. A human -- ideally a qualified physician for medical opinions, or an accredited attorney or VSO for legal strategy -- remains in the loop.

**Specificity:** The tool is built for veterans law, not general conversation. It understands the difference between 38 CFR Part 3 (adjudication) and Part 4 (rating schedule). It knows what 'benefit of the doubt' means under 38 USC 5107(b). It can distinguish between a direct service connection, a secondary connection under 38 CFR 3.310, and an aggravation claim.

The VA itself already uses an internal AI system called 'Exam Data Science' to review C&P exams for completeness and validity. Any veteran-facing tool should meet at least that standard of rigor.

## :tools: How to Use AI the Right Way

AI is a research and drafting tool -- not an autopilot for your claim. Here's how to use it effectively without putting your case at risk.

**Start with research, not drafting.** Use AI to identify relevant 38 CFR sections, understand rating criteria for your condition, and find BVA decisions where similar claims were granted. Build your understanding before you start writing anything.

**Draft, then verify.** Let AI help you organize your personal statement or structure a nexus letter framework. Then verify every citation, every diagnostic code, and every legal standard against the actual source. ecfr.gov for regulations. The BVA decision database for case law. The M21-1 for adjudication procedures.

**Never upload unredacted documents.** Strip your SSN, date of birth, and full name before pasting anything into an AI tool. If the tool doesn't have automatic PII redaction, do it yourself first.

**Pair AI with human expertise.** The best outcomes come from veterans who use AI for research and then work with an accredited VSO or attorney for strategy and submission. A phone call to your representative is worth a thousand AI-generated opinions. A letter backed by solid, verified evidence is worth ten thousand.

## :medal: The Bottom Line

The claims process was never designed to be navigated alone, and AI doesn't change that fundamental reality. What it does change is the speed and depth of your research. A veteran who spends an hour with a well-built AI tool can understand their rating criteria, identify relevant BVA precedent, and draft a personal statement that would have taken days of manual research.

But speed without accuracy is worse than no speed at all. A fabricated citation doesn't just waste your time -- it poisons your file. A leaked medical record doesn't just violate your privacy -- it can't be taken back.

> [!check] Choose your tools carefully. Verify everything. And remember that the Three Pillars -- current diagnosis, in-service event, and medical nexus -- don't change just because AI is in the picture. The standard is still 'at least as likely as not.' The evidence still has to be real. The only question is whether the tool you're using helps you meet that standard or makes it harder.

title: Secondary Service Connection: The Claims Strategy Most Veterans Miss
excerpt: Your service-connected condition may entitle you to additional ratings for secondary conditions. Here's how to identify and file these claims effectively.
author: Chris Combs
body: Secondary service connection is one of the most underutilized paths to a higher combined rating. If a service-connected condition causes or aggravates another condition, that secondary condition can also be service-connected under 38 CFR § 3.310.

title: VA Math Explained: Why 50% + 30% Doesn't Equal 80%
excerpt: The combined ratings table confuses almost every veteran. Here's a plain-English guide to how VA calculates your overall disability percentage.
author: Chris Combs
body: If you've ever been told your combined VA disability rating and thought 'that math doesn't add up,' you're not alone. VA uses a specific formula called the 'combined ratings table' that works nothing like regular addition. Understanding this system is essential for every veteran.

title: The Duty to Assist: What VA Owes You (And What to Do When They Fail)
excerpt: VA has a legal obligation to help develop your claim. Here's what that means in practice, how to spot violations, and how to use them on appeal.
author: Chris Combs
body: One of the most powerful tools in a veteran's arsenal is the VA's duty to assist. Under 38 U.S.C. § 5103A, VA is legally required to make reasonable efforts to help you obtain evidence needed to substantiate your claim. When they fail in this duty, it can be grounds for remand on appeal.

title: M21-1 Updates You Missed: January 2026 Changes Decoded
excerpt: A plain-language summary of the latest M21-1 adjudication manual changes and their practical impact on pending claims.
author: Chris Combs
body: VA quietly updated several sections of the M21-1 adjudication manual in January 2026. These changes affect how raters evaluate certain conditions and process specific claim types. Here's what changed and what it means for your pending claims.

title: BVA Grant Rates by Issue Type: 2025 Data Deep Dive
excerpt: Analyzing Board of Veterans' Appeals decision patterns to identify which claim types see the highest success rates at the Board level.
author: Chris Combs
body: Understanding BVA grant rates can fundamentally change how you approach a veteran's appeal strategy. Not all claim types are created equal at the Board level — some issues have significantly higher success rates than others, and knowing these patterns gives you a strategic advantage.

title: Building a Bulletproof Nexus Letter: AI-Assisted Research
excerpt: How to leverage AI research tools to identify the strongest medical and legal evidence for your veteran clients' nexus letters.
author: Chris Combs
body:
A nexus letter can make or break a VA disability claim. It's the critical link between a veteran's current condition and their military service. But too many nexus letters fail because they lack specificity, don't address the right legal standard, or miss key supporting evidence.

Here's how to use AI research tools to build nexus letters that actually hold up under VA scrutiny.

title: 38 CFR § 4.10 Medication Rule: The Ingram v. Collins Saga
excerpt: The ongoing battle over how VA rates disabilities managed by medication — and why every VSO and attorney needs to understand this evolving standard.
author: Chris Combs
body:
One of the most consequential regulatory battles in veterans law right now centers on a deceptively simple question: should VA rate a disability based on how the veteran functions WITH medication, or WITHOUT it?

The answer has massive implications. A veteran whose hypertension is controlled by daily medication might present with normal blood pressure at a C&P exam — but without that medication, their condition could be severely disabling.

The Ingram v. Collins line of cases has been pushing VA to confront this issue head-on. Here's what you need to know about the current state of play and how it affects your claims strategy.

title: Euzebio v. McDonough: What It Means for Your TDIU Claim
excerpt: A breakdown of the latest CAVC ruling and how it could affect veterans currently pursuing Total Disability based on Individual Unemployability. The Court found that VA must consider all evidence of employability, not just the most recent exam.
author: Chris Combs
body:
The Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims issued a significant decision in Euzebio v. McDonough that reshapes how TDIU claims must be evaluated. At its core, the Court held that the Board of Veterans' Appeals erred when it relied solely on a single VA examination to deny TDIU, while disregarding lay evidence and vocational assessments that supported unemployability.

This matters because many veterans have their TDIU claims denied based on a brief C&P exam that doesn't capture the full picture of how their service-connected disabilities affect their ability to work. The Euzebio decision reinforces that VA must weigh ALL competent evidence — including the veteran's own testimony about their functional limitations.

Key takeaways for practitioners:

• VA cannot cherry-pick a single exam over contradicting evidence
• Lay statements about work capacity carry real evidentiary weight
• Vocational expert opinions should be obtained and submitted
• The "substantially gainful employment" standard requires a holistic analysis

If you have a pending TDIU claim or a recent denial, review whether the decision adequately addressed all evidence of unemployability. This case gives you strong ground for a supplemental claim or Board appeal.
